questions.prompt: Why do strong players usually castle early?
options: It tucks the king away to safety and connects the rooks
It puts a rook in the center
It frees up the queen to attack
questions.explanation: Castling is the only move in chess that moves two pieces at once. It tucks the king behind a wall of pawns and lets the rook enter the game on a more active file. Two birds, one move.

options: The king has already moved, the rook has already moved, pieces are between them, OR the king is currently in check
Only when the king is in check
questions.explanation: You can't castle if: the king OR the chosen rook has already moved, there are pieces between them, OR the king is currently in check. (Note: the king can castle OUT OF, through, or into check is also forbidden if any of those squares is attacked.)

questions.prompt: White to castle kingside. Tap the square the king ENDS UP on after the castling move.
questions.explanation: Kingside castling: the king moves e1 -> g1, the rook moves h1 -> f1. The king ends on g1. The rook ends on f1. Queenside castling would have been e1 -> c1 and a1 -> d1.
